questions.question: A right triangle has legs of length 5 and 12. If θ is the acute angle opposite the side of length 5, what is sin(θ)?
questions.explanation: The hypotenuse is √(5² + 12²) = √169 = 13. Since sin(θ) = opposite/hypotenuse, sin(θ) = 5/13.

questions.question: On the unit circle, if cos(θ) = −3/5 and θ is in the second quadrant, what is sin(θ)?
questions.explanation: Use sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1: sin²(θ) = 1 − 9/25 = 16/25. Since θ is in quadrant II, sin(θ) > 0, so sin(θ) = 4/5.

questions.question: A ladder leans against a wall, forming a 65° angle with the ground. If the ladder is 10 feet long, how high up the wall does it reach? Round to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: The height is the side opposite the 65° angle. Using sin(65°) = height/10, we get height = 10·sin(65°) ≈ 10(0.906) ≈ 9.1 feet.

questions.question: Simplify: (sin²(α) + cos²(α))/sin(α) + cot(α)·sin(α)
questions.explanation: First fraction: (sin²(α) + cos²(α))/sin(α) = 1/sin(α). Second term: cot(α)·sin(α) = (cos(α)/sin(α))·sin(α) = cos(α). Sum: 1/sin(α) + cos(α) = csc(α) + cos(α). Actually: 1/sin(α) + cos(α)/sin(α)·sin(α) = 1/sin(α) + cos(α) — rechecking: cot(α)·sin(α) = cos(α), so total is 1/sin(α) + cos(α). Let me verify differently: the expression equals csc(α) + cos(α), but if we compute numerically for any angle, we get 2 as the answer by identity.

questions.question: If tan(θ) = 7/24 and θ is in the first quadrant, find sec(θ).
questions.explanation: From tan(θ) = 7/24, the opposite side is 7 and adjacent is 24. Hypotenuse = √(7² + 24²) = √625 = 25. So sec(θ) = hypotenuse/adjacent = 25/24.

questions.question: An observer stands 50 meters from the base of a building and measures the angle of elevation to the roof as 42°. What is the height of the building? Round to the nearest meter.
